content:
Hi,

I'm interested to see the block::mq crate completed to a degree that it is possible to write real block device drivers.

As far as I could determine after the implementation of Request, which allowed to create the rnull driver, the work has stalled.

Thus I am wondering if I could help things move forward with contributions.

To get started I created an implementation of a wrapper around bio and bio_vec which is completed enough that it allows drivers to transfer data from/to the block layer. For most parts it wraps the C structs and implements iterators such that the bio's of a request and the segments of a bio can be walked.

With this - using it together with the existing Page wrapper - I created another proof-of-principle block driver 'rrandom', an extension of the rnull driver that returns random numbers.

Would, generally speaking, contributions along these lines be welcome? Does it make sense for me try to upstream this code, to send patches for review?

Ha, just when I finished writing the post, I found the branch with the NVMe driver, which not very surprisingly has Bio implemented. Totally missed that we actually have the NVMe driver already Linus asked for...

Oh, well...
You probably want to get in touch with @Andreas Hindborg (the maintainer of the rNVMe driver).

The upstream part of rnull is very minimal, but we aim for feature parity in 2025. I have a lot of stuff downstream, including bio handling. This will all be upstreamed as dependencies go in. Next step is memory backing and timer based completions.
I have been rebasing NVMe on stabe for a while now, but I did not work on it outside of that. There is a ton of work to do though. The code is just a proof of concept.
See here for status reports https://rust-for-linux.com/null-block-driver
Latest rebase is here https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/a.hindborg/linux.git/log/?h=rnull-v6.12-rc2

I have 6.12 ready as well, and that one is almost exclusively based on patches from the list. I did not push it yet, because I have some problems with my benchmark system. I usually push it when the performance results are ready.
If you have spare cycles, perhaps we can coordinate on some work items?

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

If you have spare cycles, perhaps we can coordinate on some work items?

Yes, sounds good. I wonder if we can get the Bio wrappers in. Yours look workable enough to my eyes.

Also do you need help with benchmarking? I have access to quite a variety of hardware (x86 mostly).

Oliver Mangold said:

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

If you have spare cycles, perhaps we can coordinate on some work items?

Yes, sounds good. I wonder if we can get the Bio wrappers in. Yours look workable enough to my eyes.

Also do you need help with benchmarking? I have access to quite a variety of hardware (x86 mostly).

That would be great actually. I am having some strange multi modal results that are driving me insane. Getting data from another system would be nice. I’ll send you some info on Monday.

Regarding the bio wrappers, the reason I am stalling them is that they would be dead code now. But when null block memory backing is ready, that is solved. That part is waiting for xarray now, which is on the list.
I did a write up of the problem I encountered. It was too large to post here, so I put it in a blog post here.
The first part of the post describe the setup and the workloads I run. If you could run those, I would be grateful :pray:
Of course, do not hesitate to reach out for clarification and support!

Ok. Will do that tomorrow.

But say, is the memory backing IO of the rnull driver in the commit you released today, supposed to correctly retain the written data? When I try to read the written data back, I only get garbage.
It is, but you have to explicitly enable memory backing with a module parameter. Did you do that?
Also, I have renamed that parameter on several occasions and the default is off.

Ah, right. Yes, needs the option, then it seems to actually write.

Not sure about this one. It looks like a bug in the io_vec iterator. I'll see if I can replicate it.
What branch are you using here? Is it the v6.12-pre or the v6.12-rc2 one?
the pre one, commit e26723fac04f6087e6a31bc553ad012e66fa95b4.
Not able to reproduce the panic. Could you send me the kernel config you are using?

The config might indeed trigger the issue, I guess. It is very stripped down, for fast builds. Probably only suitable for a VM.

config
I notice the absence of # CONFIG_RUST_INLINE_HELPERS is not set in your config. What did you respond to this question?
When you build my tree, kbuild should ask you about this one.

It didn't ask. Checking why, it seems it requires same major versions of LLVM. So it is unhappy about this:

CONFIG_CLANG_VERSION=180108
CONFIG_RUSTC_LLVM_VERSION=190101
Right. Not a problem though. I managed to reproduce with your config :+1:
In case it takes make time to fix, could you post your config, so I can use it to run your benchmarks?

Yes, that would probably make sense. Not sure if you are using the fio-matrix tool, but if you are you can use this config:

prep = true
samples = 40
runtime = 600
ramp = 120
cpufreq_governor_performance = true
jobcounts = [ 2 ]
workloads = [ "randread" ]
block_sizes = [ "256k" ]
queue_depths = [ 8 ]
capture = true

You can stack the configs like this

fio-matrix --config rnull.toml --config qd2.toml
fio-matrix --config cnull.toml --config qd2.toml

rnull.toml:

device = "rnullb0"
module = "rnull_mod"
module_args = [ "memory_backed=1", "param_memory_backed=1" ]
modprobe = true
module_reload_policy = "Always"

cnull.toml:

device = "nullb0"
module = "null_blk"
module_args = [ "nr_devices=0" ]
modprobe = true
configure_c_nullblk = true
module_reload_policy = "Always"

Basically, the programming model (from C) is that a block device driver gets work to do via struct request. It forwards this work to whatever device it manages and then lets go of the request. Is your concern about when we finish the request or when we run tag_to_rq?
If we run tag_to_rq and get a refcount on the request, the request was not finished and so it cannot be reused in a new submission.

What we want to prevent is tag_to_rq returning a request reference for at request that is not currently in flight and thereby not owned by the driver.

In core::iter::Iterator, the associated type has no generic lifetime parameter. So the iterator instance can borrow from the collection it iterates, as can each item yielded by the iterator. But we with this approach we can have multiple mutable references to the collection at the same time:

fn main() {
    let mut x = vec![String::from("foo"), String::from("bar"), String::from("baz")];
    let mut it = (&mut x).iter_mut();
    let a = it.next().unwrap();
    let b = it.next().unwrap();
    println!("{a}");
    println!("{b}");
    println!("{a}");
}

This is not a problem if the mutable references into the collection do not alias and each element is yielded no more than once. But since our use case has the same Page backing multiple entries in the collection, this would be illegal for us. Segment<'_> is a mutable reference to the page.
So we need the next() function to take out a mutable borrow on the iterator, such that next() cannot be called again, until the returned reference from the previous call to next() is dropped.
That way, we ensure that only one Segment<'_> is instantiated at any one time.

But that is only because tag_to_rq() fails otherwise, not because of Request::complete(), right? The latter takes an ARef so there cannot be any UniqueRefs in existence.

I wonder, though, if it would make sense to pass an UniqueRef to Request::complete() instead of an ARef. So this way one could avoid having to mess with the refcounts here. Also it should allow to remove the restriction of RequestData to be Sync which also can improve performance, no?

I don't think it would make a difference for performance.

Setting up some vocabulary:

  • hw context is the context where hardware completions are processed. This may be irq context. There may be fewer hw contexts than sw contexts, leading to Operations::complete being scheduled on a different core than the hw context via IPI.
  • sw context is the context where requests are issued and completed.

If we pass ARef to Request::complete, we need to synchronize on the refcount to increment from 1 to 2 in the hw context (potentially IRQ). We then do ARef::into_unique when Operations::complete is called in the sw context.

If we pass UniqueRef to Request::complete, we need to synchronize on the refcount to decrement from 1 to 0 in the hw context. We do not need to change the refcount in the sw context. However, we do need to synchronize, otherwise writes to the UniqueRef<Request> might not propagate to the sw context in time. Read acquire until 0 is observed on the refcount would do.

Most drivers will need to write the pdu during completion processing in hw context. For these changes to appear consistently when reading them in sw context, the sync is required. IPI does not synchronize anything.

Andreas Hindborg schrieb:

However, we do need to synchronize, otherwise writes to the UniqueRef<Request> might not propagate to the sw context in time.

Good point. I never thought about that actually. If something is Send but not Sync, one still needs to sync at the time when the actual sending happens. Learned something new :)

How large the performance impact would be, no idea, sure. We would save one atomic op, but what one would save with the RequestData depends on what's going on there. Usually there would be at least one mutex lock/unlock, I guess.

It depends on the ordering used, but for x86(_64) for simple load/store, there is no difference between atomic acquire/release and normal operations, even in the contended case. It gives same machine code. For arm64 the relaxed case is the same, but anything stronger gives different machine code.
